title: Composting toilet urine diversion: separating and using liquid safely
subtitle: Why urine diversion matters, how urine diversion toilets work, and safe dilution ratios for use as plant fertiliser
excerpt: Urine diverting composting toilets separate urine from solids at the toilet bowl. This single design change dramatically speeds solid composting, eliminates most odour, and produces a nitrogen-rich liquid that, when correctly diluted, is an effective plant fertiliser. This guide covers the diversion mechanism, safe pathogen management for the liquid, legal position in England and Wales, and the dilution ratios for garden use.
sourceNotes: Stockholm Environment Institute guidance on ecological sanitation (EcoSan). WHO Guidelines on the Safe Use of Wastewater, Excreta and Greywater (Volume 4). Environment Agency EPR (Environmental Permitting Regulations) guidance on composting toilets. Welsh Government guidance on composting toilet planning permission. Centre for Alternative Technology guidance on urine diversion.

glossaryTerms.definition: A toilet design where the front section of the bowl routes urine to a separate collection vessel or drain, preventing it from mixing with the solid waste. Mixing urine with faeces creates the ammonia odour and increases moisture content, which slows composting and attracts flies.

Fresh human urine is sterile and contains nitrogen (as urea), phosphorus, and potassium in plant-available forms. Diluted 1:10 to 1:15 with water, it is a balanced liquid fertiliser. The WHO class it as low-risk for pathogen transmission when applied to soil at least 1 month before crop harvest.

content.text: In a standard composting toilet without

, urine and faeces mix. Urine is 95 percent water; the combined waste is too wet for aerobic composting, compacts, and produces strong ammonia odour. A urine diverting toilet routes the urine away, leaving the solid waste to compost in a drier, better-aerated environment.

The bowl has two zones separated by a ridge. The front zone (urine) drains via a tube to a collection container or to a soakaway/drain. The rear zone (solids) drops into the composting chamber. Men must sit to urinate to direct the stream to the front zone. Women's anatomy directs urine naturally to the front zone when sitting. The front zone drain must slope at least 30 degrees and be 20 mm internal diameter to prevent blockage with urine scale (struvite) build-up.

Fresh urine is effectively sterile. It develops ammonia odour within 24 to 48 hours as bacteria convert urea to ammonia. For garden use, urine can be stored in a sealed 25-litre container and used within 1 week, or diluted immediately and applied. For long-term storage, adding a small amount of lactic acid (vinegar) prevents ammonia development. A household of four produces around 6 to 8 litres of urine per day.

guidelines: dilute 1:10 to 1:15 with water before applying. Apply to the soil around plant roots, not to leaves or edible parts. Do not apply within 1 month of harvest of root vegetables or leafy crops that contact the soil. Urine from a person taking prescription medications should not be used on food crops; some drug metabolites are pharmacologically active and plant-available. The WHO rates undiluted, freshly collected urine as low-risk for helminth transmission (risk category D/E).
Legal position in England and Wales

A composting toilet at a UK home needs an Environmental Permit if any effluent enters the ground. Most urine-diverting systems that drain to a soakaway need a bespoke permit rather than a standard exemption. Planning permission is also needed, with some exceptions for farm or off-grid buildings. Check with your local planning authority and the Environment Agency before installing.

attrs.symptom: Urine diverter drain blocked with white scale

content.text: Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) crystallises in urine drains in hard-water areas. Dissolve with a dilute citric acid or vinegar solution left in the drain for 30 minutes, then flush. A monthly vinegar flush prevents scale build-up. Increasing the drain slope angle to 45 degrees reduces struvite deposition.
